event: The Arrest of Christ in the Garden of Gethsemane
symbols: white-robed Christ amid darkness — the Light of the World surrounded and seized by the powers of darkness
torches and lanterns — the artificial light that the forces of evil need because they cannot bear the true light
Judas pressing close to Christ — the intimate betrayal, the kiss that weaponizes love
encircling armed figures — the worldly power that believes it can contain the divine
deep surrounding blackness — the 'hour and the power of darkness' (Luke 22:53)
description: A dramatically dark composition in an arched format. Christ stands at center in a luminous white robe, His face visible in three-quarter profile, rendered with loose, expressive brushstrokes. A figure leans close to Him — likely Judas. Around them, soldiers and guards press in from all sides, their forms barely distinguishable in the deep darkness. Torches and lanterns cast flickering golden-yellow light that catches armor, faces, and weapons. The overwhelming darkness swallows the edges of the scene, concentrating all attention on Christ's illuminated figure.
theological_description: Goya's visceral rendering of the arrest in Gethsemane strips the scene to its essential theological drama: light versus darkness. John 18:3-12 records that Judas came with lanterns, torches, and weapons — a detail Goya makes the visual core of the composition. The irony is profound: they bring artificial light to seize Him who declared 'I am the light of the world' (John 8:12). Christ's luminous white garment makes Him the sole source of true radiance in the painting, visually enacting John 1:5: 'The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it.' The moment depicted fulfills Jesus's own words: 'This is your hour, and the power of darkness' (Luke 22:53). Yet even in captivity, Christ retains sovereign dignity — John's Gospel emphasizes that He stepped forward and identified Himself ('I am he'), causing the soldiers to fall back (John 18:6). Goya's rough, almost violent brushwork communicates the raw physicality of the arrest while preserving the theological paradox: the one being seized is the one who holds all things together.

scripture_refs.reason: John's account of the arrest in the garden, including lanterns, torches, and Jesus's sovereign 'I am he'
Matthew's account of Judas's betrayal and the arrest
'This is your hour, and the power of darkness' — the theological darkness dominating the composition
The light shines in the darkness — Christ's radiance amid the surrounding night
